text: © 2012 - 2018 NRT Trainings, LLC O b j e c t i v e s o f this NRT C o u r s e By the end of this course, the student should be able to: 1. Define the law of reciprocal inhibition and the law of symmetry to their clients and state the physiological basis of how NRT actually resets the muscular tonus. 2. Palpate through loose-fitting clothes and on non-lubricated skin and locate any trigger point/trigger band areas by touch and patient observation in the zones taught in this class. 3. Demonstrate all of the application methods taught in this class. 4. Demonstrate different application angles to reset the muscles more completely. 5. D e m o n s t r a t e t h a t t h e y can apply The Rules f o r O p t i m a l Results as an NRT t h e r a p i s t . 6. D e m o n s t r a t e t w e a k i n g f o r each t e c h n i q u e . 7. List the regional or global contraindications with particular application methods and angles of applications for each of their clients.

T o t h e b e s t of o u r k n o w l e d g e , p a r t o f NRT is t h e m o s t c o m p r e h e n s i v e , m o s t efficacious, a n d easiest t o apply of all c u r r e n t t h e r a p i e s based on Sherrington's Second Law (the l a w o f reciprocal i n h i b i t i o n ) a n d t h e o t h e r p a r t is a c o m p l e t e l y n e w application based o n Pfluger?s Law of S y m m e t r y . Charles S h e r r i n g t o n w o n t h e 1932 Nobel Prize f o r physiology. The book, The Integrative A c t i o n o f t h e Nervous System, published in 1906, w a s a collection of his lectures t h a t w e r e delivered at Yale University t w o years earlier, He a n d Edward Liddell w e r e responsible f o r d i s c o v e r i n g t h e m y o t a t i c reflex in w h i c h a muscle w i l l start t o t i g h t e n if it is s t r e t c h e d b e y o n d

text: delivered at Yale University t w o years earlier, He a n d Edward Liddell w e r e responsible f o r d i s c o v e r i n g t h e m y o t a t i c reflex in w h i c h a muscle w i l l start t o t i g h t e n if it is s t r e t c h e d b e y o n d a certain p o i n t . Sherrington's first l a w gave us the k n o w l e d g e of d e r m a t o m e s in w h i c h e v e r y p o s t e r i o r spinal nerve r o o t is linked t o a p a r t i c u l a r area o f skin. Sherrington's second l a w is t h e l a w o f reciprocal i n h i b i t i o n . T h e r e are several references to t h i s l a w in t h e T e x t b o o k of M e d i c a l Physiology by G u y t o n a n d Hall. This b o o k is t h e bible f o r m e d i c a l s t u d e n t s in t h e United States. Simply put, w h e n a muscle is activated, t h e a n t a g o n i s t muscle is s i m u l t a n e o u s l y inhibited. So w h e n y o u contract y o u r a r m biceps muscle, t h e t r i c e p s is partially t u r n e d off. Lawrence W o o d s first c a m e across this concept n o t w h i l e o b t a i n i n g his college degrees, b u t w h i l e he w a s in massage school a n d also being t r a i n e d in n e u r o m u s c u l a r t h e r a p y by Paul St. John several decades ago. Part o f NRT is applied in such a w a y as t o cause

text: John several decades ago. Part o f NRT is applied in such a w a y as t o cause m o r e t h a n a spinal reflex action w h i c h has o n l y a b r i e f i n h i b i t i o n effect. This is k n o w n as t h e i n d i r e c t reset in NRT w o r k . Sandy Fritz, w h o has written many of the training books and manuals used in massage schools in the United States, was the head instructor at his school. She demonstrated this principle of reciprocal inhibition several times in her advanced training courses. The one he remembers the most was her universal reset for the muscles surrounding the shoulder joint. Since he did not understand all of the nuances of how to apply reciprocal inhibition, rarely was he successful with using this method, mostly he was not.

It is n o t based on w h e t h e r a m u s c l e is actually w e a k or s t r o n g w h i l e b e i n g t e s t e d b u t on t h e reflex action of t h e n e r v o u s system. Each muscle is placed in a passive h a l f - c o n t r a c t e d position a n d t h e n t h e t h e r a p i s t i n t r o d u c e s a v e r y b r i e f a n d m i l d j e r k to s t i m u l a t e t h e muscle reflex. text: many or all of the trigger points were eliminated. The problem was that there was no consistency to whether or not this phenomenon would happen. He thought that surely someone had noticed this before and had written about it. He could find no references to this sudden release of the trigger points ín any of the literature. While intrigued, he did not understand why it only happened occasionally. H e also did an e x t e n s i v e search on t h e i n t e r n e t . Lawrence f o u n d o n l y a c o u p l e o f teachers t h a t h a d d e v e l o p e d s o m e f o r m of t h e r a p y based at least partially on t h e l a w o f reciprocal i n h i b i t i o n . W h i l e he did receive i n s t r u c t i o n o r studied t h e manuals f r o m t h e m , he w a s n o t satisfied w i t h t h e degree o f response f o r a t o t a l r e s e t t i n g o f t h e muscle t o n u s o r t h e reliability o f t h e response. W o r k i n g w i t h t h e i r m e t h o d s , he w a s n o t h a p p y w i t h t h e large percentage o f clients w h o h a d little to no response. So he set o u t t o f i n d o u t w h a t w a s missing in t h e c u r r e n t d a t a b a s e in o r d e r to make this a safe, reliable and quick a p p r o a c h to achieving o u t s t a n d i n g t h e r a p e u t i c results in t h e clinic. It was this disappointment that led him to explore further to see if something could be done to develop a systematic way of applying the law of reciprocal inhibition in a rapid way and

text: It was this disappointment that led him to explore further to see if something could be done to develop a systematic way of applying the law of reciprocal inhibition in a rapid way and with a high rate of success. He owes much to his clients for their patience and time in developing Neural Reset Therapy. Along the way, Lawrence had six insights that revolutionized and transformed our understanding of how to work with the nervous system of the body and apply it in a practical and reliable way.

Through trial and error, y o u w i l l select t h e ones t h a t w o r k f o r t h a t client. It m a y be j u s t o n e o r several. O t h e r teachings i n v o l v e d w i t h a p p l y i n g t h e law o f reciprocal i n h i b i t i o n did n o t seem t o t a k e i n t o a c c o u n t t h a t an i n d i v i d u a l muscle o r a set o f muscles w i t h a c o m m o n action m a y have f i b e r s a t t a c h e d at d i f f e r e n t angulations to create d i f f e r e n t m o v e m e n t p a t t e r n s w h i c h m a y r e q u i r e m o r e t h a n

text: Individual muscle or set of muscles with common action may have fibers attached at different angulations to create different movement patterns which may require more than one application angle. For example, the external rotators of the hip all laterally rotate the hip but due to their attachment sites cannot be reset completely by applying stimulus with one angle (Figs 1-3). Trigger points and bands that develop in part of the muscles may be cleared only by repositioning the limb body and applying stimulus at a different angle than what may be used to clear other parts. The same is true for the sternocleidomastoid muscle. It cannot be completely released by one single angle of approach (Fig 4). The unique multi-planar positionings that have been developed will create much success in releasing the target muscles at ST-36, among others.

text: The u n i q u e m u i t i - p l a n a r p o s i t i o n i n g s t h a t have b e e n d e v e l o p e d w i l l create m u c h success in releasing t h e t a r g e t muscles. The third insight which creates more success in resetting the tonus in some muscles is to divide them into proximal and distal sections as if they were different muscles. The technique to reset the proximal or upper part of the biceps brachii closer to the shoulder is not the same to use for the distal portion closer to the elbow.

The fourth innovation that Lawrence brought to this work is the principle of randomness or unpredictability. While experimenting and developing this therapy, he came to realize that the reset usually came much faster and more dependably when introducing a bit of randomness or unpredictability with the movement patterns or the recoil tapping. For example, instead of performing a smooth arc of flexion of a joint, veering a little bit from side to side while performing the arc movement works much better with the reset (Figure 1). There is no need for introducing a shock to the system, just a little bit of surprise. Direct your client to look away from what you are doing. This helps to flood the spinal integration center with so much information that a reset is activated. Note that this is not a spinal reflex action as some may think (Ref: Lawrence 2012).
section: 6. D e m o n s t r a t e t w e a k i n g f o r each t e c h n i q u e .

The fifth innovation that Lawrence brought to this work was the concept of the Simple Way for making indirect resets easy. For most of the application methods, the therapist does not really need to know the antagonist muscte(s) to activate. If the therapist knows the origin and insertion of the dysfunctional muscle, it is usually fairly easy to figure out what movement pattern that muscle performs. With the Simple Way, one positions that muscle in a passive half-contracted state and asks the client to perform the opposite movement pattern. The therapist provides some resistance. Or the therapist performs the movement patterns of the d y s f u n c t i o n a l muscle w h i l e t h e client resists. Or both t r y t o m o v e in o p p o s i t e w a y s . This c o n c e p t is easier t o d e m o n s t r a t e t h a n to describe. The Simple W a y cuts o u t h a l f o f t h e m e n t a l gymnastics usually r e q u i r e d w i t h using t h e l a w of reciprocal i n h i b i t i o n . T h e sixth innovation,
to f u r t h e r enable t h e p r a c t i t i o n e r t o reset t h e muscle t o n u s q u i c k l y a n d reliably, w a s in t h e discovery of T w i n Reset. T w i n Reset is a novel a p p l i c a t i o n o f Pfluger?s Law o f S y m m e t r y . Pfluger?s law can be defined as: If t h e s t i m u l a t i o n is sufficiently increased e n o u g h , m o t o r reaction is m a n i f e s t e d , n o t o n l y b y t h e engaged or s t i m u l a t e d side, b u t also in

text: Pfluger?s law can be defined as: If t h e s t i m u l a t i o n is sufficiently increased e n o u g h , m o t o r reaction is m a n i f e s t e d , n o t o n l y b y t h e engaged or s t i m u l a t e d side, b u t also in t h e same muscles on t h e contralateral side. Basically, when one takes the same muscle on the other side of the sagittal plane and has the client contract that muscle while the therapist is placing a stimulus into the system, a reset is done for the target muscle. For example, if the client has a hypertonic right upper trapezius, the therapist would have the client contract the left upper trapezius while applying a stimulus to the left upper trapezius. This will cause a reset to a normal tonus state for the right upper trapezius.

The NRT therapy can be used on a wide variety of clients in all ages and differing physical conditions. It has worked well with frail 85-year-olds, restless 5-year-old children, and athletes. With a little practice, you will get results just as good, if not better, as you did with other therapeutic approaches, but in a fraction of the time. In many instances, clients will report back to you that certain chronic conditions that they have had for years and that you have worked on for years are suddenly much better. Expect the unexpected. With these six innovations, these laws of physiology can work for practically everyone. It is in only rare circumstances to meet a client in which this therapeutic approach will not work. It is true that with some clients, the therapist may have to work a little harder and longer to find that particular combination that will work to release the muscles.

The cerebral cortex and brain). The signal is interpreted in an integrating center and an exiting signal is sent through an efferent motor nerve to cause those muscle fibers to tighten. Another exiting signal is sent through another motor nerve to inhibit other muscle fibers. If we activate the mechanoceptor properly, the target muscle fibers are reciprocally inhibited thus forced to let go of its tension.

When NRT (Neurological Relaxation Technique) is done correctly, the muscle tone is reset or down-regulated, no longer painful to palpation and is lengthened. This reset is not a phenomenon that lasts for a few seconds like what happens in an normal reflex arc activation but it is a permanent reset that lasts as long as no other precipitating factors are involved.

text:
Muscle mechanoreceptors include:

* Muscle spindles, which respond to changes in muscle length (Golgi tendon organs are not located within muscles).
* Golgi tendon organs, which respond to tension in muscle tendons.
* Aponeurotic ligaments' end-organs, which respond to stretch and contraction of the aponeurosis.
* Sustained-end organs of ligaments.

Pacinian corpuscles, which respond to pressure changes (e.g., acceleration or deceleration), are located:

* In skin
* In deep tissues, in response to rapid and sustained pressure

Contraindications

1. Clients with a history of easy bruising - no recoil tapping on muscles.
2. Clients with osteoporosis - no recoil tapping on ribs or rapid movement of joints (j o i n t s ).
3. Clients with a history of physical trauma - no recoil tapping or reflex jerking without full consent from the client. Even with consent, if the patient's psychological demeanour changes after a reset, do not use those application methods.
4. Clients with unstable joints or joint replacements - therapists should take caution on which angles of application and the amount of force that the joints can tolerate without dislocation or traumatization.
The static holding application method with mild to moderate force by the client is the safest.

5. Clients with a history of recent surgery, unhealed sprains and strains, or active inflammation - do not stress that particular area. With our NRT approach, we can reset the Qi (Qì) at ST-36 ( Shéntiànzús Ĭ ) and CV-6 ( Zhòngwèinǎo) points, but it is still necessary to apply caution and avoid stressing areas with recent surgery or inflammation.

Recoil t a p p i n g w i t h t h e side o f t h e hand, t i p s of fingers, o r reflex h a m m e r The concept of recoil is to very briefly tap into a contracted muscle or tendon and very quickly taper off the application force. The opposite of a recoil tap would be a karate master chopping through a pile of bricks or boards while maintaining speed and force all the way through and out the end. In NRT, we want to quickly decrease the force as soon as we have hit the tissue. W h e n t a p p i n g w i t h t w o h a n d s o r t w o h a m m e r s , strike t h e tissue simultaneously, n o t a l t e r n a t e l y like in t a p o t e m e n t . We may apply just a few recoil taps or a series of asynchronous taps. The more chronic the problem and the older the client, more taps will be required. A l w a y s tell y o u r client w h a t you are about t o do a n d indicate t h e area y o u are a b o u t to strike

Direction arrows were purposefully left o u t of the pictures so that you can d r a w your o w n that make sense t o y o u . A f t e r having taken many courses, most of the t i m e the arrows in those manuals w o u l d seem to indicate an inaccurate direction. To eliminate any confusion on y o u r part, d r a w your arrows in during this course or while watching the DVD. 2. All pictures depict t h e reset f o r t h e right side except as noted or w h e n a bilateral t w i n reset is shown. 3. Do n o t restrict yourself to t h e hand and arm positions portrayed in t h e pictures f o r t h e therapist. W h a t w o r k s f o r one therapist, m a y n o t w o r k f o r y o u . These positions are suggested only. 4. Version 2 or 3 of a particular technique may w o r k b e t t e r than Version 1. You m a y prefer to mostly use one version over others. Just remember, there are different versions w h e n the one t h a t you prefer t o use m a y n o t be possible w i t h a particular client due to a contraindication or a body size issue. Sometimes it is just f u n to mix it up. 5. If a particular area does n o t seem to be able to reset, remember to revisit t h e Rules f o r Optimal Results. There has to be an underlying pathology or near panic state to prevent a reset f r o m occurring. 6. Amazingly enough, even if b o t h t h e target muscle and t h e muscle you are activating to reset t h e target muscle are both in need of resetting their muscle tonus level, NRT w i l l still work. 2 0 1 2 - 2 0 1 8 NRT Trainings, LLC Lower Body ? Zone 5 Palpate the Vastus Lateralis (p. 306 ?-310). Think of this muscle of having t w o zones ? anterior fibers and lateral fibers. Palpate t h e a n t e r i o r f i b e r s zone as a strip a b o u t 2 inches w i d e i m m e d i a t e l y lateral t o t h e Rectus Femoris. The lateral fibers zone would be the rest of the muscle occupying most of the lateral thigh which is partially covered by the lliotibial Tract (p. 324 ? 325) ?a fascial band connected to the Tensor Fasciae Latae (p.324 ? 325}. Note: For the lateral portion of the Vastus Lateralis, we are primarily focused on resetting the medial rotators and abductors of the hip which refer trigger point pain down into the lliotibial Tract. Note: It is m o r e t i m e efficient t o p a l p a t e Zones 4 ? 7 b e f o r e d o i n g any resets. Muscle and fascial band: 1. Vastus Lateralis (p. 306 ?310) Action: extend the knee 2. lliotibial Tract (p. 3 2 4 ? 325) Actions: assists in transferring power from the TFL to flex, medially rotate, and abduct the hip Reset S e q u e n c e 1. Z o n e 11 Palpate the mid ? muscle belly of the Psoas Major which is part of the Hiopsoas (p. 332 ? 335) per the detailed information (p. 334) in the anatomy book. Palpate with the hip flexed to provide slack in the abdominal area. We will not be palpating the other portion of the Wliopsoas known as the lliacus. Superficial to this muscle will also be the Abdominals (p. 209 ? 212). It is more efficient to palpate Zones 11, 12, and 13 together and then reset as needed. Note: For t h o s e w h o are less detail o r i e n t e d , t h e Psoas M a j o r does n o t have to be palpated. It is tight a n d or s h o r t on a l m o s t e v e r y o n e . Do t h e reset f o r e v e r y o n e . This muscle plays a role in l o w e r back pain. M u s c l e s : 1. lliopsoas (p. 332 ? 335) Actions: flex a n d laterally r o t a t e t h e hip, flex t h e t r u n k , a n t e r i o r l y t i l t t h e pelvis, a n d l a t e r a l l y flex t h e l u m b a r spine 2. Rectus A b d o m i n i s (p. 2 0 9 ? 212) Actions: flex the vertebral column and posteriorly tilt the pelvis Reset S e q u e n c e 1. lliopsoas Reset Palpate after each reset. Lower Body ? Zone 12 Palpate the internal and external obliques (pp. 209-212) lateral to the Rectus Abdominis and also where they attach to the lower ribs, iliac crest, and the inguinal ligament. It is more efficient to palpate Zones 11, 12, and 13 together and then reset as needed.

Muscles:

1. External and internal obliques (pp. 209-212) Actions: flex, laterally flex, and rotate the spinal column
2. Iliopsoas (pp. 332-333) Actions: flex and laterally rotate the hip, flex the trunk, anteriorly tilt the pelvis, and laterally flex the lumbar spine
3. Rectus Abdominis (pp. 209-212) Actions: flex the vertebral column and posteriorly tilt the pelvis

Reset Sequence

1. Abdominals (External and Internal Obliques) Reset - Version 1 or 2 Palpate after each reset. If unable to reset completely, go to #2.
2. Iliopsoas Reset - see Zone 11 Palpate after each reset. If unable to reset completely, go to #3.
3. Abdominals (Rectus Abdominis) Reset - Version 1 or 2 - see Zone 13

Version 1 Position: Client is sitting w i t h f e e t on f l o o r a n d spinal c o l u m n c o m f o r t a b l y f l e x e d w i t h hands hugging o p p o s i t e shoulders. T h e r a p i s t has o n e h a n d o n u p p e r t h o r a c i c spine a n d t h e o t h e r h a n d holding a r e f l e x h a m m e r . A c t i o n : Client a t t e m p t s t o lightly e x t e n d t h e spinal c o l u m n as t h e t h e r a p i s t tries to press i n t o flexion w h i l e s i m u l t a n e o u s l y and r a n d o m t y recoil t a p s on t h e paraspinals, especially t h o s e c o r r e s p o n d i n g t o t h e same h e i g h t as t h e section of Rectus A b d o m i n i s t h a t is in need o f reset. For example, t a p on t h e paraspinals on t h e sacrum and l u m b a r area f o r reset of t h e l o w e r Rectus A b d o m i n i s . Note: Be sure to have client use their Erector Spinae muscles instead of pushing with their hands or forearms against the thighs. Too tight or thick clothing in this area can decrease the effectiveness. A n o t h e r variation could be d o n e w i t h o u t t h e h a m m e r w i t h t h e t h e r a p i s t pushing t h e back i n t o t h e d i r e c t i o n o f f l e x i o n as t h e c l i e n t a t t e m p t s to e x t e n d .

text:
Lower Body Zone 14: Palpate the area lateral to but not including the lumbar spine and superior to the posterior iliac crest up to the inferior border of the 12th rib. Note that the Lumbar Erector Spinae Group (pp. 196-199) and the Latissimus Dorsi (pp. 71-73) are attached to the thoracolumbar aponeurosis in this area. For time efficiency, palpate Zones 14 and 15 together before doing any reset. Often, resetting one resets the other.

Muscles:

1. Quadratus Lumborum (pp. 207-208) Actions: elevate the pelvis, laterally flex and help to extend the vertebral column
2. Lumbar Erector Spinae group (pp. 196-199) - lliocostalis Actions: extension and same side lateral flexion of spine
3. Latissimus Dorsi (pp. 71-73) Actions: extend, adduct, and medially rotate the shoulder

Reset Sequence:

1. Erector Spinae and Transversospinalis Group (Lumbar) Reset – Version 1 or 2 Palpate after each attempt. If unable to clear pain completely, then do step #2.
2. Latissimus Dorsi/Teres Major Reset – Version 1 or 2 Palpate after each attempt

text:
2012 - 2019 NRT Trainings, LLC PSIS Reset (Zone B6) Position: The client is supine with the ankle planted in flexion and toes flexed as much as possible on the target side. The therapist places finger tips of one hand under the flexed toes and the other hand cradling the distal leg to act as a stabilizer. Action: The client attempts to hold position as the therapist pulls up the toes into the direction of extension. Repeat several times. Note: You may wonder how this works through the Law of Reciprocal Inhibition. The PSIS area is part of the extensor chain of muscles and fascia. We ask the client to contract the flexor chain of muscles of the ankle and toes. There are numerous mechanoreceptors in the ligaments too. When we briefly and precisely hyperstimulate the mechanoreceptors of the muscles and ligaments for flexion, the extensor chain is down-regulated to a more normal tonus state.

Do w e have t o do all o f t h e zones o f t h e b o d y each t i m e ? One of the reasons for less than optimal results from any bodywork therapy is that the entire pattern of dysfunction created throughout the body from an injured or restricted area is usually not completely treated. For example, an ankle problem can cause the person to move abnormally and thus cause other areas to be irritated and/or tightened down. If the therapist only treats the ankle and perhaps the calf and foot, the rest of the body may not be able to break out that pattern of dysfunction.
